reply_text:
Oh, please do explain how the reality of Protestant oppression of Catholics is more complex than portrayed.

If anything the show greatly muted it.

Especially considering only 30 years earlier the penal laws against Catholics were abolished. 
You know the penal laws, right? 
The system of legal exclusion that was objectively more harsh than both South African apartheid and Jim Crow laws USA, and lasted longer than both combined.

video_title: House of Guinness | Official Trailer | Netflix
reply_text:
​You raise an important point — the penal laws were indeed a devastating system of exclusion, stripping Catholics of land ownership, education, civil rights, and political power for over a century. They were harsher and longer-lasting than many modern forms of oppression, and they profoundly shaped the Irish social and religious landscape. I don’t deny that reality, and the show could have gone even further in portraying just how damaging they were.

When I said the history is “more complex,” I didn’t mean to diminish Catholic suffering, but rather to note that any period of history involves multiple layers: not all Protestants were oppressors, some Protestants actively opposed the penal laws, and some Catholics themselves navigated the system in surprising ways. Complexity doesn’t cancel injustice; it just reminds us that history rarely fits neatly into “all good vs. all bad.”

As a Christian, I also see persecution in a paradoxical way: it is always wrong and sinful when people are deprived of justice — yet God often uses persecution to purify and strengthen His people. The New Testament repeatedly shows this pattern: “Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness” (Matthew 5:10); persecution scattered the early church so that the gospel spread (Acts 8:1–4); Paul himself suffered greatly yet said it produced perseverance and hope (Romans 5:3–5). Even in Irish history, Catholic faith endured and often deepened precisely under pressure, just as Protestant dissenters in England or Baptists in early America grew stronger through marginalization.

So yes, the penal laws were a grave injustice. But in God’s economy, even oppression can be mysteriously transformed into spiritual resilience and a deeper witness to the gospel. That doesn’t excuse the sin of persecution — it only magnifies how God can redeem suffering.

reply_text:
The Guinness family's roots are believed to be in the Gaelic, Catholic clans of Ireland. The most popular theory is that they descended from the Magennis clan from County Down. It's very likely that at some point, his family converted to Protestantism, a move often referred to as "taking the soup," to avoid the Penal Laws and thrive in a society dominated by the Protestant Ascendancy.
​Arthur Guinness was a shrewd businessman and a part of that Protestant Ascendancy, but he was also a philanthropist who invested heavily in Dublin and was a strong advocate for Catholic Emancipation. His story shows that Irish identity is not a monolith; it's a blend of different religions, political views, and historical circumstances. So, it's fair to say his Irish roots are legitimate, even if his religion and politics were different from the majority.

video_title: House of Guinness | Official Trailer | Netflix
reply_text:
​@UlTiMaTz400 not true that Arthur Guinness funded Protestant paramilitaries. This is a common historical misconception.
​Here's a breakdown of the facts:
​Arthur Guinness died in 1803. The most well-known Protestant paramilitary group, the Ulster Volunteer Force (UVF), was founded in 1912. The modern paramilitary group with the same name was formed much later, in 1966. The timelines simply do not align.  
​Political Stance: Arthur Guinness was a Unionist and opposed the 1798 Irish Rebellion, believing Ireland should remain under British rule. However, he was also a strong advocate for Catholic emancipation and supported politicians who sought to repeal anti-Catholic laws. His political views were complex and not aligned with the later, more militant forms of Loyalism.  
​Funding of the UVF: The confusion likely stems from a well-documented donation made by a descendant of Arthur Guinness, Lord Iveagh, to the Ulster Unionist Council in 1913. This donation, made to the political wing of the Unionist movement, helped fund the UVF's arms and ammunition to resist Irish Home Rule. This action was highly controversial and led to boycotts of Guinness products by some nationalists.

video_title: House of Guinness | Official Trailer | Netflix
reply_text:
It was Lord Iveagh, Arthur's great grandson that funded the Ulster Volunteer Force (UVF) in 1913. This action, to resist Home Rule by force, cemented the family's anti-Irish reputation among many nationalists.

After Arthurs death, as the family gained wealth and titles, they became more explicitly pro-British and staunchly opposed to the Irish Home Rule movement.

This idea that Arthur Guinness came from the Norman nobility is fiction... his dad was the land steward from the Bishop of Cashel... while working in his stepmother's pub Arthur and his father received an inheritance from the Bishop which allowed him to open the original Lexlip brewery.  He was Church of Ireland which made him more socially mobile,  but he was a self-made entrepreneur.

His grandparents were tenant farmers.

He did believe he was descended from nobility... but that was Gaelic Catholic nobility the Magennises of Iveagh.  That belief was incorrect.

Modern DNA testing has instead traced his family back to the McCartan family hailing from a village called Guiness in Co. Down.

(Glad to finally find a use for my transition year school project on Guinness)
